title: Jane S -Peer Interview | Chief of Staff - Ops & Special Projects
meeting_title: Jane S -Peer Interview | Chief of Staff - Ops & Special Projects

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Of course, how are you doing?
I'm doing okay.
It's been really busy, not going to lie.
We are trying to wrap up the year and finalize our 2026 strategy OKRs, performance reviews, end of quarter.
So, you know, last minute deals coming in, all that fun stuff.
The rush.
Yes, the rush.
Are you also feeling something similar or has it been more chill on your side?
Yeah, would say I would not use chill as a word to describe it, but in a good way.
mean, learning so much, you know, trying to maintain and build out a consistent process for onboarding new clients to success.
And, like, as we're simultaneously hiring new people and putting new people in these roles.
what, what, Thank
That we need in the system and in the process.
so that's a really hard problem that we're solving right now before we get into the new year where we're booked out already six weeks.
And so it's really prepping for that, like the system level.
And then simultaneously as like the company's moving really fast and we're working really hard on the product and to expose it soon to clients.
And so a lot, a lot going on for sure.
Yeah, I can see that it's not chill for sure.
Good though, not boring as well.
Not chill goes with not boring and also with learning a lot.
So, but yeah, I was excited to meet and get to know you as well.
caught up with Bridget and Marcel a bit and I know they're super excited.
So we'd love to like learn a little bit more about you and we can kind of go from there.
Yeah, sounds great.
Likewise.
Are you, are you based in the Bay?
Yes, I am actually in San Francisco near Rinkin Hill.
What about you?
Nice.
I'm in North Beach currently.
Oh, okay.
So fairly close by too.
Super close.
Yeah, not as close as Rincon Hill, but is that coming back at all?
I know it was a little quieter, Rincon Hill, right after COVID, but I know the city itself is coming back.
Yeah, yeah.
Especially with more of like the AI companies filling up downtown area, I would say at least the apartment complexes in my neighborhood, it's been getting fuller.
Yeah, it's like one of my friends, as an example, she just moved back from New York.
She was interested in my apartment, but they don't really have a lot of openings, which like a few years ago, that was a little bit unheard of.
So there's always openings.
So yeah, I think it's getting busier.
Nice.
And then where were you?
Are you from here?
Did you move here recently?
Yeah, so I'm from, I'm originally from the Bay Area, born and raised, went to school in Berkeley, then moved over to SF right after college.
And I've just been here ever since.
Yeah, kind of boring.
Nice.
It's to beat, though.
It's hard to beat, so I can't blame you.
Yeah, especially with family around, too.
It's hard to be far away from them.
Yeah, no, that helps.
That helps.
Cool.

For the sake of time, because we have, let's see, 24 minutes here, if you're all right with it, I just have a few questions I would love to learn a little bit more about where you were at prior and maybe some problems or challenges that you faced.

I would say the main thing for me that I'm just super curious about people in general, so I'm wondering if you can share an example of a time where you had a really undefined problem or area of work, right, and what you did to kind of get to stability and consistency.
And the reason I'm asking is because, like, that's a lot of what we're
So dealing with now is things that don't exist, that we stand up, but then need to make them consistent, repeatable, and stable.
And it's pretty critical for us.
And so curious if you've experienced in the past other companies and then digging into that a bit more.
Yeah, absolutely.
So the story that comes top of mind is one of the biggest initiatives I drove at Rasa was redesigning our strategic planning process and setting our company strategy.
So just for context, when I joined Rasa last year, there was no really strategy that existed.
And I don't consider three goals.
This is business strategy?
is like overall business strategy?
Correct.
Entire like company strategy, like company priorities.
Like, and I don't consider, you know, like an ARR number and a couple of other metrics as a strategy.
That's goals.
Like strategies, how do you get to those goals?
Right.
So, and so like one of my biggest, one of the biggest things in my scope was, hey.
Can you help, can you define the company strategy?
Of course, like bringing the cross-functional leaders, bringing ELT along the journey.
So last year, what I did was, I think the first step is always to listen and gather input.
So I ran, initially, I ran feedback sessions with each of the cross-functional leaders to get their input.
And I also worked with the head of people to analyze their past engagement survey results.
So they're just gathering data to understand, like, how our 2024 strategy landed with the organization.
The key pain points I was consistently hearing that I wanted to address were leaders felt that they had not been involved in the process.
Our 2024 OKRs were seen as, like, way too unrealistic.
And there just wasn't a shared understanding of how we were going to hit our numbers or, like, how our numbers have been set.
So as a storing point, what I like to do is, there are many frameworks out there.
This is just one.
This This
I like to use frameworks as a starting point because it helps structure the thoughts, the input, and the data.
So for this specific case, I use the Good Strategy, Bad Strategy Framework by Richard Rommelt, and it breaks it down into three components.
it's like diagnosis, guiding principles, and actions.
So starting with diagnosis, that means understanding our challenges today.

So again, like I worked with teams to run analyses to just assess past performances, what are the trends, identifying bottlenecks, for example, looking at when loss analyses, gone calls, funnel metrics, layering in competitive analysis by partnering with a PMM team.
So a very cross-functional effort, all of this to build a view on the biggest gaps and challenges that we're facing, which we boiled it down to two.
The first was there was a steep learning curve for anyone to try and use our product.
We're super technical and therefore longer time to value.
And secondly, no one in the market really knew about us.
So then the next step for me was, okay, what are our guiding principles or like what's our approach to overcoming these challenges?
So.

So at our ELT offsite and team meetings, I led a series of workshops and discussions to define those priorities for the year, as in what are the strategic focus areas that we really need to double down on that will address these gaps, critical gaps, and that will create the most leverage for us.
What is that impact that we need to be delivering on and defining those outcomes as well?
So like first step is defining what's the problem we're trying to solve for, and secondly, it's like what is the outcomes and how are we going to do it?
And I think the important element here is rather than just listing everything you want to accomplish, you want to include trade-offs that need to be made.
I think strategy is just about like what you're saying yes to and what you're saying no to.

So ultimately, where we landed on from all this was prove value faster as a thesis of our strategy, meaning how can we create more wow moments in our user journey so that people experience the power of our platform?
More quickly and therefore accelerate adoption and revenue.
The two guiding principles.
Principles under Proof Value Faster were making our products simpler and easier to use, and secondly, building brand awareness and champions.
So then once this had been set, I worked with ELT to translate these into success metrics.
Then I partnered again with the cross-functional leaders.
How did you translate them into tangible success metrics that then the team could go execute on?
So we defined our strategic pillars, you could say, right?

And then it was about, I led a brainstorming exercise to give you more detail in terms of, like, if we were to say we achieved this well, like we did this well by the end of 2025, what would that success look like?
Like, what is that one metric?
Don't give me five, give me that one metric.
If we hit, like, if we scored high on this metric, then we would consider it was a success.
So a lot of putting ideas together, voting, and discussion, and that's how we aligned on.
On one, like really tying it back to like that specific pillar per se.
Yeah, totally makes sense.
What was the, what was the metric for shortening time to value, making the product easier to use for someone who's less technical?
Yeah.
So the making the product easier to use, that was, we measured it by weekly active users to see like how many of them are able to easily download, install, and train a model using our product.
And so that training the model is what we define as a success metric.
Like we don't consider it to be a success if all they do is just download our product and don't actually use or build with it.
And so what we did was we measured the weekly active users and see if there was, like, there was like an upward, upward trend to it.
Yeah.
Got it.
So it's just so I, so I'm understanding correctly, you're tracking towards weekly active users, but the, like the, the critical moment or the aha moment is when they act.
And so how are you getting from, you know, we got a hundred more weekly active users, whatever the number is, to these weekly active users took these actions and actually did what we wanted them to.
And then how do we go find more of them?
So your question is, how do we increase the number of users?
Yeah, like what did you, so the goal is there, the weekly active users and that you want them to activate in this way, right?
How did you actually work towards that?
Like what were the steps that you need put in place or the actual work to?
Yeah, so I mean, it's multifaceted, right?
So I think from the product standpoint, we released a product where it was easier for them to get their hands on it.
So we put something on our website, like it's a vibe coding experience.
Then the next step of that user journey.

It be, it leads them, like once they successfully play around with our sandbox, our playground product, that's what we call it on the website, that's very easily accessible and you just type away in natural English, then it leads, then if you want to continue the project or like you want to build a little bit more, then it leads to our other product, which you download, install, and then you successfully train a model.
And then we also started measuring like how many usage we get from like that, that playground product that we released earlier this year, as well as to see like what that correlation would look like.
And then from a marketing and PMM, DevRel standpoint, we started reaching, we led campaigns for specifically targeting like the technical teams, as well as like the business users of our ICP.

We also started building a lot more content as well, which is what attracted me to growthx in terms of like white papers or doing like blog posts on some of like the technical innovations we're doing and hosting like community.
community meetups as well of ML engineers and conversational AI designers within the hottest spots of the U.S.
and in Europe.
It makes sense.
What were the biggest, I guess, mistakes you made during that process?

So it sounds like you were heavily involved in kind of the scoping, shaping, and prioritization of the business strategy, creating it, getting everybody aligned on it, and then executing against it or leading the teams to execute against it.
I guess, what were the biggest mistakes you made?
And they're tied to like the biggest challenges that you faced.
Yeah, so I think, in hindsight, what I would have done is, there was, so you know how I mentioned trade-offs that needed to be made?
Originally, the way we approached it was, these are like the biggest...
Initiatives that we need everyone's teams focused on.
then these are like minor initiatives where we had a couple of people staffed on it.
Those initiatives, there was, it did not move the needle as in there, rather than spending 10% of people's efforts on the smaller initiatives, we decided to, it wasn't moving the needle.
So like the effort wasn't producing, wasn't translating into outcomes.
And so like my- What's an example of that?
Sorry to interrupt.
An example of that was, there was another like trial product that we were working on for our interface product.
And that one was, it's basically like a free version of them to try out the interface of our platform.
There wasn't that much traction to it because there was already a lot of fundamental product issues that we were dealing with, with like the inter, the actual interface product.
The중it The중it će��서.
The중it moonit The중it
So instead of working on this free trial version, in Q2, we ended up pivoting.
So we took the resources on that free trial product and redirected it into our interface product to make it stronger and to address the bugs as well as the customer feedback.

So instead of spreading ourselves too thin, my lesson was we need to get the core products, do it really well, and make sure they're addressing 80%, let's say, of customers' feedback before we try and launch other different versions of that product, if that makes sense.
Yeah, yeah, I think that makes sense.
Make sure we're actually solving problems that our users and customers care more about and don't spread ourselves too thin.
Let's focus on the areas that are driving the most results and driving the most results are downstream of solving actual problems.
And also looking at the market too, what our competitors are doing?
They have a really easy to use, downloadable interface, whether it's part of their platform or separately.
So how do we make sure that's a table stake feature?
don't even consider that to be very innovative.
So let's bring that to the finish line before we try to do a spin-off of a free trial version and spend a lot of go-to-market resources and some engineering resources there.
Got it.
Was that a mistake?
Sorry, was that a mistake that like the company had made prior to you or a mistake that like you were able to learn from as you were going through this process of like prioritizing leadership?
The latter, the latter.
It was a mistake and it was something, a lesson that we took away from ELT in terms of being more explicit on like what are we saying like absolute no to?
Like instead of doing, instead of like spreading it like, oh, like maybe like 10% here, 10% here, like.
Let's just pour the majority of our resources into where we think it will move the needle the most and, like, get that to the finish line before, like, the next thing.
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Interesting.

And then I think the other component to this, too, was at least initially in the beginning, like, we rolled it out, the company's strategy and OKRs, and we communicated, had a Notion page, like, had all the documentation there, right, socialized it.

But I think what I learned from Q1 was we need to keep reinforcing it, and not just on a quarterly basis, but literally, like, on a monthly, even, like, maybe weekly, like, having a ripple effect and empowering our managers to also keep tying everything back, the work, to our company strategy as a North Star for this year.

So in Q1, like, beginning of the year, big rah-rah moment, rolled it out, and then when we came to Q2, it's, like, some people may have forgotten the details or, you know, not everyone is always remembers everything.
So,

What I implemented was, okay, now starting Q2, at every single all-hands, ELT, one of us, will talk about the strategy, reinforce why it's important, why it matters, and also showcasing the progress we're making, how we can come up and talk about their major initiatives and so forth.
Love that.
No, that's super, it's really hard to do.
It's like a different part of the brain, right?
It's like prioritizing and understanding where the business should go, but it's also like how do we reinforce this to the team, continue to like set the vision, excite them.
But it's like a different level of abstraction where you're like closer to the work and like the work being done and like iterating on that.
Okay, that's super helpful and interesting.
How did you all like actually help managers or, you know, the teams stay aligned to the priorities outside of like the all-hands, like reinforcing in the all-hands?
Like what processes were in place or weren't that you had to put in place?
Like make sure people were staying on top of these goals and that their work was aligned.
I mean, I think there's multiple pieces to this.
maybe starting from kind of like the top level, I restructured our ELT meetings and ELT offsites where we did quarterly retros.
We had never done that before.
And I created dashboards with Ops and Finance to track the progress against our OKRs as well as leading indicators.
So I think like as part of the process, you need to have a pulse on how we're doing and using data to see like, are we falling behind?
Why are we falling behind?
Looking at leading indicators as well.
So not just the OKRs, but the surrounding KPIs too, and actually discussing them.
And so kind of like empowering the ELT at the top with how we're doing and giving them that visibility.
I think the second layer is the management team.
And so we had monthly management group meetings before I joined RASA, but they always ended up being canceled.
That was the feedback I heard because there was like nothing.
But I was like, actually, I that's a very valuable time for us to make sure we're on the same page and that we're enabling them and that they are able to ask us ELT questions too.

And so that's where anytime there were like pivots or changes or like feedback that we were hearing, we would use that as a grounds for discussion and as well as asking, holding the managers accountable for the big rocks that ladder up to the OKRs.

So like asking them to present to ELT and to in front of the other leaders and talking about like lessons they learned, what worked well, what didn't, talking about like where the collaboration was strong and so forth.
And so I think that was a really good way to keep them accountable and to also give them visibility.
And then, of course, like documentation, I think is everything.
So we use Lattice to track our OKRs as well as our team OKRs.
So your teams were filling that out like on a quarterly basis and meeting.

Like me and the people team, as well as some of the yield team members, we meet with like the managers and one-on-ones and team meetings to like discuss like how this past quarter went and like making sure it's all documented down in terms of the progress that they're making.
And then ultimately it ends up like present, we present some of this stuff to the board too.
So involving them in like the board updates and investor preparations as well.
Nice.
That's super interesting.
If, and this is hard to do because we always have like a bias, but like if your boss was to rate you on a scale of one to 10, like your performance, what do you think it would be and why?
I would say she would give me a nine.
I would say that because.
As I came in and there was chaos and it was crazy since the first week I joined Rasa.
Every week there was something changing.
There wasn't a foundational structure or processes in place.
There was no strategy in place.

I honestly think strategy and like having that vision, we didn't even have like a proper vision, is like the number one thing that orients all the projects, initiatives, programs, whatever, like the teams to march in the same beat.
So I came in and I was able to definitely add much more structure and actually deliver like a strategy, not just a strategy, but like a two-year North Star as well as like an overall company vision.
And I think that really helped to align all the efforts across the company.
I also served as, I would say, I mean, I serve as the extension of her.
So my ultimate goal is like to save her time, like my CEO time.
So really being able to.
Dive into the details and know all the details, but also bringing it up to like that 10,000 feet level for her.
It's something that I spent a lot, I still spend a lot of time doing, whether it's as an example, you know, if we're going to hit sales targets or not, like what are all the deals in play?
Like why are these deals at risk?
Why did we lose these deals?
And like having all that information, compiling it, synthesizing it for her.
I think the areas where I can be doing better is each leader has their own style and preference.
And so she does, the leader who I serve today is very different from the leader who I served in the past.
My current CEO, she really likes to be very prepared, like as in having all her talking points, having like pre-reads done from way before.
So she has time to like digest and process.
She's not the type of person to like riff.
Go ad hoc, like speak publicly, right?
Go
So I think that was an area that took some time for us to get on the same page with because for me, I like to move really fast and there's like 10 million things that we're trying to do.
So just being better at meeting her where she needs in terms of like that additional preparation that she required.
Nice.
Yeah, it's hard.
It's because like there's a way to process information and learn it and be able to talk about it and present it.
But then ultimately, like it's who you're working with, right, or presenting it to is the deciding factor, like how it lands or not or how they process it.
And so completely understand.
And like, I feel like that's a constant, not even challenge.
It's just a constant thing here.
Like you're balancing different leaders, the CEO, but also like we're still series A and scrappy.
And so a lot of what we do is client-facing, right, as well.
And so it's able to take all this context.
That is crazy and chaotic.
Understand it, learn it, understand how to communicate it, and then also understand who we're speaking to, right?
And what they need to hear or what would be beneficial to them.
And that has been one of the most challenging parts, but it's like an amazing skill to learn.
Yeah, absolutely.
Speaking in their language, but also having to translate it over, it's definitely not easy.
Sometimes it gives me headaches, to be honest, but here we are.
Yeah, no, I hear you on the headaches.
Cool, fun questions for the last three minutes.
What do you love doing outside of work?
I like to move my body.
I like to push myself.
So as an example, I like to snowboard.
like to be on the mountains.
I also like to run as well.
So I used to do long distance running, but I broke my foot and I also sprained my ankle.
So ever since then, it's, I haven't been able to do like super long distances, but even like a few miles outside always clears my head and gives me that endorphin boost.
Yeah, I think like healthy mind, healthy body, they're all connected to each other.
So just really taking care of my physical health, of course, mental too, but keeping my body moving and just being outside is something that I have really ingrained in my day to day.
Lately, to be honest, it's been tough because work has just been absolutely bonkers.
But, you know, usually I have a very like kind of like regimen, like a routine that I like to follow.
Yeah.
Nice.
What do you think makes you like that type of person?
You're that's relatively unique, right?
Like driven, motivated, want to be healthy, to feel better, want to push yourself in like ways outside of just like exercise, right?
Like clearly with work, you're ambitious, you're like striving for things.
Like what, what do you think like drives you towards?
I think what intrinsically drives me towards is I always, I'm the type of person where, it's a double-edged sword, I'm always like, what can I be doing better?
What's like the 1% progress I can be making every day?
I think there's a balance to it where you, it's good to also just pause and enjoy the moment and be proud of how far you've come.
And that's something I'm working on.
But I think the way I was raised probably, it stems from my childhood, just like, how can I do a little better?
Like, how can I be better?
I think there's just so much opportunity out there, right?
Like, especially with LLMs, like the internet has so much information.
You can literally learn anything, any skills you've got.
It's crazy.
There's no excuse anymore.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
And so my kind of motto in life is just like, stay hungry, stay curious.
It doesn't, not just at work, but yeah, I mean, there's just so much to learn both from like reading or.
Just from observing, from talking to people, and yeah, I think that's what makes me really excited.
And in terms of like being healthy, I actually was on the opposite end of the spectrum, like in school, where I just ate very poorly, didn't work out and whatnot.
I just felt very slow.
It just didn't feel good to me.
And I think like once I started eating a lot healthier, switching to whole foods and just working out more consistently, I just felt better.
So I was like, yeah, why not?
I keep doing it.
Yeah.
Hard to argue with feeling better.
It's like the best.
Amazing.
We're at time and I have to jump, but this was awesome.
Thank you for being so open.
Like it was great getting to know you and hearing about your experience.
was great.
Yeah.
Likewise.
Thank you so much for your time.
I appreciate all the questions and yeah, I enjoyed our conversation.
So thanks George.
Amazing.
Anything I can help with or any questions you have in the meantime, feel free to like, I think you had my email on the invite.
So happy to.
Answer anything or chat again if you're unclear on things, whatever it is.
Okay.
Sounds great.
Thank you, George.
Take care.
Yep.
You too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Peer interview with Jane Suk for the Chief of Staff role.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=99.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [At Rasa, Jane Suk built a company strategy from scratch using the "Good Strategy, Bad Strategy" framework, defining a "Prove Value Faster" thesis to overcome a steep product learning curve and low brand awareness.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=257.0)
  - [The strategy was measured by weekly active users (WAU) who successfully trained a model—the key "aha moment"—and executed via a new web-based "playground" sandbox and targeted marketing campaigns.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
  - [Key learnings: focus resources on core products before launching spin-offs, and reinforce strategy constantly (e.g., in all-hands) to maintain alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=734.0)
  - [Jane Suk self-rates as a 9/10, citing her ability to add structure to chaos and synthesize complex info for the CEO; her growth area is adapting to a leader's specific communication style.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1232.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Lack of Strategic Direction

  - [Rasa lacked a formal strategy, relying on a few high-level goals instead of a defined plan for achieving them.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=270.0)
  - [Feedback from leaders and engagement surveys revealed key pain points:](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)
      - [Exclusion from the strategy-setting process.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)
      - [Unrealistic 2024 OKRs.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)
      - [No shared understanding of how goals were set or would be met.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)

### The Solution: A New Strategic Framework

  - [Jane Suk led the development of a new strategy, using the "Good Strategy, Bad Strategy" framework to structure the process.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=353.0)
  - [**Diagnosis:** Identified two core challenges:](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=376.0)
      - [**Steep Learning Curve:** A highly technical product led to a long time-to-value.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
      - [**Low Brand Awareness:** The company was unknown in the market.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=395.0)
  - [**Guiding Principles:** A series of workshops led to the "Prove Value Faster" thesis, with two pillars:](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=412.0)
    1.  [**Product:** Make the product simpler and easier to use.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=463.0)
    2.  [**Marketing:** Build brand awareness and champions.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=463.0)
  - [**Action Plan:** Translated principles into a single, measurable success metric per pillar.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=487.0)
      - [**Product Metric:** Weekly Active Users (WAU) who successfully train a model.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
          - [**Rationale:** Training a model is the key "aha moment" that proves value, not just a download.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=546.0)
      - [**Execution:**](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=625.0)
          - [**Product:** Launched a web-based "playground" sandbox for a low-friction trial, funneling users to the full product.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=644.0)
          - [**Marketing:** Ran targeted campaigns and created content (white papers, blogs, meetups) for technical and business users.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=690.0)

### Learnings & Process Improvements

  - [**Resource Focus:**](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=734.0)
      - [**Mistake:** Spreading resources across minor initiatives (e.g., a free trial for the interface product).](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=762.0)
      - [**Correction:** Pivoted resources to fix fundamental bugs and address feedback on the core interface product.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=812.0)
      - [**Lesson:** Master core products first; spin-offs are a distraction when fundamentals are weak.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=858.0)
  - [**Strategic Reinforcement:**](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
      - [**Mistake:** A one-time strategy rollout in Q1 led to details being forgotten by Q2.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
      - [**Correction:** Implemented constant reinforcement in all-hands meetings and empowered managers to tie daily work to the strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
  - [**Accountability & Cadence:**](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1062.0)
      - [**ELT:** Restructured meetings to include quarterly retros and used dashboards (Ops/Finance) to track OKRs and leading indicators.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1086.0)
      - [**Managers:** Revitalized monthly meetings for alignment and accountability, requiring managers to present progress to the ELT.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1128.0)
      - [**Documentation:** Used Lattice for OKR tracking and involved managers in board updates.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1188.0)

### Self-Assessment & Personal Drivers

      - [**Strengths:** Added structure to chaos, delivered a clear strategy and vision, and synthesized complex info for the CEO.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1232.0)
      - [**Growth Area:** Adapting to a leader's specific communication style (e.g., a CEO who prefers extensive pre-reads).](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1347.0)
  - [**Personal Drivers:**](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
      - [**Motivation:** Driven by a constant desire for 1% daily improvement, balanced with learning to pause and celebrate progress.](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
      - [**Motto:** "Stay hungry, stay curious."](https://fathom.video/share/_BEfrxx_okb2P46LyRSMgmKE1Ar9PyRU?tab=summary&timestamp=1596.0)
